subject: [PATCH v3 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

reviews.author: Doug Anderson
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI event signal call sdei_event_signal() is not properly synchronized, which could lead to a race condition when delivering an NMI to a CPU.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested using kernel-doc format for function comments in this file, noting that all other functions follow this convention and it wouldn't introduce any issues.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch lacks a necessary memory barrier (smp_rmb()) to match the existing smp_wmb(), which is required for correct ordering of operations.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi,

On Sun, Jun 14, 2026 at 7:36 PM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Nice to have all the details in the function comment. Any reason why
you didn't use kernel-doc format? Nothing else in this file does, I
guess, but it doesn't seem like it would be a problem to start the
trend... ;-)

---

Don't you need a smp_rmb() before that, to match with the smp_wmb()?

-Doug

2026-06-16.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture, using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) protocol. The goal is to enable interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked, which is necessary for certain debug and observability features such as system backtraces and crash dumps. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI events delivered by firmware to reach the target CPU without modifying the interrupt mask/unmask hot path. This patch series does not add a hard-lockup detector but provides a way for the backtrace and crash-stop paths to actually reach a masked CPU.
